failed.title: ISSUE_001: Percentage (20%) is the controlling affordable housing standard
failed.match: PASS if the draft establishes the 20% percentage as the controlling standard for affordable housing (not a fixed unit count), so that if unit counts change during design development the affordable obligation adjusts proportionally. FAIL if the draft uses only a fixed unit count (e.g., 100 or 106 units) without tying it to the 20% percentage.

failed.title: ISSUE_001: Recalculation mechanism if unit counts change
failed.match: PASS if the draft includes a mechanism or provision for recalculating the number of required affordable units if total residential unit counts change during design development or phase rescoping. FAIL if there is no such recalculation mechanism.

failed.title: ISSUE_002: Allocates who performs remediation
failed.match: PASS if the draft assigns responsibility for performing the environmental remediation work (whether Developer, City, or a third party) for Parcel 016 (petroleum hydrocarbon contamination) and Parcel 017 (engineered barrier). FAIL if the draft is silent on who performs remediation.

failed.title: ISSUE_009: Identifies LEED certification timing vs. Substantial Completion
failed.match: PASS if the draft identifies the issue that LEED Gold certification (Resolution condition #6) typically occurs months after building completion due to post-construction verification, and that tying LEED certification to Substantial Completion could disrupt the phasing timeline (since the next phase's commencement deadline runs from Substantial Completion). FAIL if this timing issue is not identified.

failed.title: ISSUE_009: Separate LEED certification deadline post-completion
failed.match: PASS if the draft establishes a separate deadline for achieving LEED Gold certification after Substantial Completion (e.g., 12 months post-Substantial Completion or similar reasonable period). FAIL if there is no separate LEED certification deadline.

failed.title: ISSUE_009: Consequences for failure to achieve LEED Gold
failed.match: PASS if the draft specifies consequences for failure to achieve LEED Gold certification (e.g., cure periods, acceptance of alternative certifications/equivalents, financial penalties, or liquidated damages). FAIL if no consequences are specified.

failed.title: ISSUE_011: Clarifies force majeure cap application (per-phase)
failed.match: PASS if the draft clarifies that the force majeure extension cap applies on a per-phase basis (or otherwise addresses the cascading delay risk across phases), rather than leaving an ambiguous project-wide aggregate cap. FAIL if the draft simply recites '18-month aggregate' without clarifying per-phase vs. project-wide application.

failed.title: ISSUE_012: Identifies tax abatement limited to Phase I only
failed.match: PASS if the draft identifies or flags that the property tax abatement (75%/50%/25% over 12 years) applies only to Phase I improvements, creating a financing asymmetry for Phases II-IV which bear full property tax burden from completion. FAIL if this limitation is not identified or flagged.

failed.title: ISSUE_012: Addresses abatement asymmetry for later phases
failed.match: PASS if the draft includes a provision addressing the tax abatement asymmetry — such as requiring the City to consider in good faith extending abatement to later phases, acknowledging that TIF reimbursement is expected to offset the absence of abatement for later phases, or otherwise flagging this as an open negotiation point. FAIL if the abatement is limited to Phase I with no acknowledgment of the asymmetry or any provision regarding later phases.

failed.title: Includes equity demonstration requirement ($85M within 60 days)
failed.match: PASS if the draft includes a requirement that Developer demonstrate committed equity of at least $85,000,000 within 60 days of the resolution (i.e., by approximately April 19, 2025), consistent with Resolution condition #1. FAIL if this requirement is omitted or the amount/timeline is incorrect.

# Development Agreement Draft — Summary of Work

## Deliverable
`development-agreement-draft.docx` — a developer-side draft of the Development Agreement for the **Lakemont Station Commons** mixed-use transit-oriented development, prepared on behalf of **Granite City Development LLC**, with bracketed drafting notes flagging cross-document conflicts and open items. The .docx has been generated via Pandoc and validated (ECMA-376 schema-valid, ZIP/XML integrity confirmed).

## Source Documents Reviewed
1. **City Council Resolution R-2025-017** (adopted Feb 18, 2025, 7–2 vote) — conditional approval and conditions.
2. **Negotiated Term Sheet** (Jan 22, 2025) — principal economic/legal terms.
3. **Construction Loan Term Sheet** (First Prairie National Bank) — up to $210M loan; lender requirements.
4. **Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP Side Letter** (Feb 10, 2025) — investor protections/consent rights.
5. **Phase II Environmental Site Assessment** (Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC, Nov 8, 2024) — environmental conditions and remediation.
6. **TIF District Summary Memorandum** (Corporation Counsel, Mar 5, 2025) — privileged City internal analysis of TIF reachability.
7. **RFP Response Narrative** (Dec 16, 2024) — Developer's project proposal.

## Agreement Structure (20 Articles)
Recitals → Art. 1 Definitions → Art. 2 Project/Development Plan → Art. 3 Property & Land Conveyance → Art. 4 Public Incentives (land write-down, TIF, infrastructure, abatement, fee waivers) → Art. 5 Environmental Matters → Art. 6 Financing & Equity → Art. 7 Performance Security → Art. 8 Construction Standards (prevailing wage, LEED, insurance) → Art. 9 Affordable Housing → Art. 10 Community Benefits → Art. 11 Phasing/Timelines/Force Majeure → Art. 12 Completion Assurance (clawback alternative) → Art. 13 Default/Cure/Termination → Art. 14 Indemnification → Art. 15 Representations → Art. 16 Assignment & Change of Control → Art. 17 Conditions Precedent → Art. 18 Lender Protections → Art. 19 Dispute Resolution → Art. 20 Miscellaneous → Signature Page → Exhibits/Schedules → Consolidated Conflicts/Open-Items Index (A–NN).

## Key Developer Positions Taken
- **No reversionary interest** (Art. 12): substituted performance security + liquidated damages + contractual repurchase option subordinate to the lender's mortgage, to satisfy the Construction Lender's prohibition on defeasible fees while addressing the Resolution's Condition 2(h).
- **TIF**: pay-as-you-go with reimbursement priority (environmental → infrastructure → Phase IV public → other); mutual "shortfall is not a default" provision; City good-faith covenant to seek TIF extension; Land Write-Down and City infrastructure treated as separate from the $48M TIF cap.
- **Environmental**: broad, uncapped, survival-bearing City indemnity for pre-existing contamination; City bears cost overruns (25–35% contingency); NFR/remediation delays treated as Force Majeure.
- **Assignment/Change of Control**: broader permitted-transfer carve-outs (internal transfers, approved capital raises, Key Person provision, 60-day cure) to accommodate the ~$58.4M later-phase raise.
- **Community benefits**: local hiring as a good-faith goal (non-default); living wage/first-source framed as tenant-covenant obligations Developer must require, not guarantee.
- **Financing-out condition** for each Phase; expanded Developer termination rights; mutual consequential-damages waiver.

## Cross-Document Conflicts & Open Items Flagged (59 drafting notes; 38 indexed items A–NN)
Highlights:
- **CRITICAL — Reversionary interest** (Resolution requires it; Lender prohibits it; Lakeshore requires consent).
- **CRITICAL — Change-of-control thresholds** (Lender [50]%, Term Sheet 50%, Lakeshore 51%).
- **MAJOR — TIF cap reachability** ($48M may only reach $38–42M with abatement per the privileged TIF memo).
- Parcel acquisition history conflict (Parcel 016: tax forfeiture 2016 vs. direct purchase 2018).
- Parcel acreage conflict (RFP 3.8/4.1/4.8 vs. ESA 4.2/4.1/4.4).
- Affordable unit count ("at least 100" vs. 106/20%).
- Equity demonstration trigger (60 days from Resolution adoption vs. from DA approval).
- Living wage & first-source hiring (in Resolution, absent from Term Sheet).
- Prevailing wage scope; insurance limits; IEPA RAP timing; force majeure cap; conditions-precedent lists; circular loan/DA dependency; Lakeshore Side Letter compatibility.

All bracketed notes are clearly marked as non-operative drafting guidance for negotiation.

**[1. Introduction and Purpose]{.underline}**

**[1.1 Authorization and Scope]{.underline}**

Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC (\"GEC\") was retained by Granite City Development LLC (\"Developer\") to conduct a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment (\"Phase II ESA\") of three parcels comprising approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) located at 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048 (collectively, the \"Site\"). The assessment was authorized by Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager/CEO of Granite City Development LLC, pursuant to an engagement letter dated August 12, 2024.

The Phase II ESA was conducted to evaluate recognized environmental conditions (\"RECs\") identified in the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment previously prepared by GEC (GEC Project No. GEC-2024-0612, dated July 19, 2024) (the \"Phase I ESA\") and to characterize soil and groundwater quality across all three parcels comprising the Site. The Phase I ESA identified several RECs associated with historical industrial operations on the property, including petroleum storage, metal finishing operations involving chlorinated solvents, and the placement of fill material of uncertain provenance on portions of the Site. The present Phase II ESA was designed to investigate these RECs through a comprehensive subsurface sampling program, including the advancement of soil borings, installation of temporary groundwater monitoring wells, and laboratory analysis of soil and groundwater samples for a range of contaminants of concern.

The investigation is intended to support the Developer\'s due diligence in connection with the potential acquisition of the Site from the City of Lakemont, Illinois, for development of a mixed-use transit-oriented project adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station (the \"Lakemont Station Commons\" project). The Site is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) under the City of Lakemont Zoning Ordinance. The Developer has submitted an application for rezoning of the Site to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) to accommodate the proposed mixed-use development. The City of Lakemont acquired the three parcels comprising the Site through two distinct transactions: Parcel 12-24-300-015 was acquired by the City through tax forfeiture proceedings in 2016, and Parcels 12-24-300-016 and 12-24-300-017 were acquired by the City through direct purchase in 2018. The Developer intends to acquire the consolidated Site from the City pursuant to a development agreement currently under negotiation.

**[1.2 Regulatory Framework]{.underline}**

The Phase II ESA was conducted in general conformance with ASTM Standard E1903-19, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and applicable Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (\"IEPA\") guidance documents, including the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program (\"VSRP\") Guidance Document (Publication 2024) and the IEPA Technical Guidance for Investigation and Remediation of Contaminated Sites. The subsurface investigation was designed to collect data sufficient to evaluate site conditions against the remediation objectives established under the IEPA Tiered Approach to Corrective Action Objectives (\"TACO\"), codified at 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 742.

The TACO framework establishes three tiers of remediation objectives for soil and groundwater. Tier 1 objectives are generic, risk-based standards applicable statewide. Tier 2 objectives are site-specific standards developed using modified exposure parameters or institutional and engineering controls. Tier 3 objectives are derived from a site-specific risk assessment. The analytical results from this investigation have been compared to Tier 1 remediation objectives for both residential and industrial/commercial land use scenarios, as well as to Class I groundwater quality standards established under 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 620.

The findings of this Phase II ESA will be used to determine whether enrollment in the IEPA VSRP is warranted for any or all of the three parcels and whether a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter can be obtained for the Site or individual parcels upon completion of any necessary remedial activities. The VSRP provides a voluntary, incentive-based mechanism for investigation and remediation of contaminated properties under IEPA oversight, culminating in the issuance of an NFR letter upon satisfactory completion of approved remedial actions.

The Lakemont Central TIF District was established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, adopted by the City Council on September 17, 2019, pursuant to the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.* The City of Lakemont is a home rule municipality with a population of approximately 87,000, exercising home rule powers under Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970, in addition to the statutory TIF authority conferred by the TIF Act. The TIF district was designated on the basis of findings that the redevelopment project area qualified as a conservation area under the eligibility criteria then applicable under the TIF Act, including findings relating to obsolescence, deterioration, inadequate utilities, and environmental contamination. The Lakemont Central TIF District encompasses the project site and a number of surrounding parcels, including the area immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station.

The parcels are to be consolidated into a single legal parcel prior to or concurrent with the land conveyance to the Developer under the forthcoming development agreement.

**C. TIF District Duration and Expiration**

**The Lakemont Central TIF District expires on December 31, 2042.** Under the TIF Act, the maximum statutory life of a TIF district is twenty-three (23) years from the date of the ordinance designating the redevelopment project area. Ordinance No. 2019-42 was adopted in 2019, and the 23-year statutory limit extends through 2042, consistent with the December 31, 2042 expiration date set forth in the establishing ordinance.

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

The task is complete. Here's a summary of what I delivered:

## Deliverable: `development-agreement-draft.docx`

A **~19,300-word developer-side draft** of the Development Agreement for the **Lakemont Station Commons** mixed-use transit-oriented development, prepared on behalf of **Granite City Development LLC**. The document is schema-valid (ECMA-376) and ZIP/XML-integrity confirmed.

### What I did
1. **Read all seven source documents** — the City Council Resolution, Negotiated Term Sheet, Construction Loan Term Sheet, Lakeshore Side Letter, Phase II ESA, TIF District Summary Memo, and RFP Response Narrative.
2. **Extracted and cross-referenced** the project facts, then systematically identified every cross-document conflict and open item.
3. **Drafted a complete 20-Article agreement** reflecting the developer's negotiating positions, with **59 bracketed drafting notes** flagging conflicts and open items, plus a **consolidated index (items A–NN)** at the end.

### Key developer positions embedded
- **No reversionary interest** (Art. 12) — substituted performance security + liquidated damages + a contractual repurchase option subordinate to the lender's mortgage, resolving the central conflict between the Resolution (which demands a recorded reversion) and the Construction Lender (which prohibits any defeasible fee).
- **TIF protections** — reimbursement priority, mutual "shortfall is not a default," City good-faith extension covenant, and treating the land write-down/infrastructure as separate from the $48M cap.
- **Environmental** — broad, uncapped, survival-bearing City indemnity; City bears cost overruns; remediation delays as Force Majeure.
- **Assignment/Change of Control** — broader permitted-transfer carve-outs and a Key Person provision to accommodate the ~$58.4M later-phase capital raise.
- **Financing-out condition**, expanded developer termination rights, and a mutual consequential-damages waiver.

### Conflicts flagged (highlights)
- **CRITICAL**: reversionary interest (Resolution vs. Lender vs. Lakeshore); change-of-control thresholds (50% vs. 50% vs. 51%).
- **MAJOR**: TIF cap reachability ($48M may only reach $38–42M per the privileged TIF memo).
- Plus: parcel acquisition history, parcel acreages, affordable-unit count, equity-demonstration trigger, living wage/first-source hiring, prevailing-wage scope, insurance limits, IEPA RAP timing, force majeure cap, conditions-precedent lists, the circular loan/DA dependency, and Lakeshore Side Letter compatibility.

A `response.md` summary accompanies the deliverable in the output directory.
